canonical_definition: Across the provided literature, imagination is most commonly described as a cognitive ability for mental simulation and visualization, particularly in the spatial domain. This is frequently specified as "spatial imagination," the ability to mentally manipulate spatial relationships or visualize the outcomes of transformations, such as a physical process or code execution. A different framing treats imagination as the generation of novel visual representations, specifically "intermediate subgoal images," to guide planning and action. Another view emphasizes its role in inferring missing perceptual knowledge, with one study noting that model performance "degrades when required to imagine a scene after changes before reasoning" (Hypo3D). The construct is operationalized through tasks like 3D visualization problems in coding, where failure to, for example, apply the Right-Hand Rule is attributed to "limited visual spatial imagination" (Can MLLMs Reason in Multimodality? EMMA: An Enhanced MultiModal ReAsoning Benchmark). A single paper also posits a causal link, suggesting imagination influences creativity.
papers: Can MLLMs Reason in Multimodality? EMMA: An Enhanced MultiModal ReAsoning Benchmark
ConvBench: A Multi-Turn Conversation Evaluation Benchmark with Hierarchical Ablation Capability for Large Vision-Language Models
PERIA: Perceive, Reason, Imagine, Act via Holistic Language and Vision Planning for Manipulation
WildVision: Evaluating Vision-Language Models in the Wild with Human Preferences

definitions.text: The ability to generate novel visual representations, specifically intermediate subgoal images, corresponding to planned sub-instructions.
definitions.paper: PERIA: Perceive, Reason, Imagine, Act via Holistic Language and Vision Planning for Manipulation

definitions.text: The latent ability to imagine the outcomes of spatial transformations or manipulate spatial relationships mentally.
definitions.paper: WildVision: Evaluating Vision-Language Models in the Wild with Human Preferences

relationships.paper: ConvBench: A Multi-Turn Conversation Evaluation Benchmark with Hierarchical Ablation Capability for Large Vision-Language Models
